text.fullText: From: Susan Pastin <noreply@adv.actionnetwork.org> To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/11/2026 8:15:07 PM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 ----------------------------------------------------- Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. The Environmental Protection Agency, No, our federal government should never have the right to allow anyone to poison. Any community’s water supply! And yes, poisoning ground water counts, and can be just as harmful! You should be thoroughly ashamed of your profits over life proposed changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. For 50 years, this provision has provided essential protection for our nation's waterways by giving states and authorized tribes the ability to review federally permitted projects that could affect their water quality. The proposed rule would significantly limit this authority by restricting reviews to only "direct discharges" into waterways, rather than allowing comprehensive assessments of broader environmental impacts. This change would weaken local communities' ability to protect drinking water sources, fisheries, wetlands, and cultural resources. Furthermore, the proposed strict deadlines and limited review scope would accelerate project approvals without ensuring adequate environmental protection. This comes at a particularly concerning time, following recent judicial decisions that have already reduced federal water protections. Water is a shared resource that sustains communities, ecosystems, and economies. Once contaminated, restoration can take generations. The current Section 401 authority provides essential safeguards that allow local communities to protect their water resources for current and future generations. If any of you have ANY decency, the EPA should withdraw this proposed rule and maintain the current authority that allows states and tribes to conduct meaningful water quality reviews. Our nation's waterways deserve comprehensive protection, not weakened standards that prioritize expedited project approvals over environmental stewardship. Obviously, you don’t care about fetus either, who can develop birth can defects through contaminated water! So much for your hypocritical “right to life” protestations! It was about controlling women all the time, wasn’t it? You would be less miserable yourselves if you stop trying to make everyone else miserable. You would be happier doing your job to do good rather than thinking of new ways to do harm. Susan Illinois
